input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided documents pertains to **manufacturing systems**, specifically **injection molding processes** as a primary use case. The system comprises **cyber-physical components** (CPS) including physical machines, sensors, actuators, and production lines, where **real-time data traces** (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow rates, and dimensional measurements) are continuously collected from the physical system. The **operational domain** is **continuous process optimization**, decision support for production planning, and **real-time monitoring** of manufacturing parameters. Key characteristics include:
  - **Heterogeneous data sources**: Sensors embedded in injection molding machines (e.g., pressure sensors, temperature probes) and external systems (e.g., PLCs, SCADA).
  - **Multi-layered abstraction**: The system is modeled using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, including **SysML** for functional/logical architecture, **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** for component-connector perspectives, and **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** for behavioral modeling.
  - **Purpose-driven design**: The system is tailored for **decision support** (e.g., optimization of continuous processes) and **production control**, with a focus on **traceability** of data traces back to their origin (processing components, models, and input data).
  - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: The system adheres to a **reference model** for digital shadows, where data traces are aggregated, abstracted, and processed by **Processing Components** (e.g., simulation or calculation models) to generate higher-level abstractions. Metadata supports further data processing in digital twin scenarios.
  - **Conformance to standards**: The modeling follows **conformance relations** (e.g., mapping of UML class diagrams to reference concepts) to ensure consistency with domain-specific requirements (e.g., injection molding constraints).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) system** provides the following **specific services** to users (e.g., engineers, operators, decision-makers) and the physical system, grounded in the documents:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - Real-time **data aggregation and abstraction** of sensor traces (e.g., pressure, temperature) from injection molding machines, enabling **continuous process monitoring**.
    - **Traceability services**: Transitive traceability of data traces back to their **processing components, input data, and models** (e.g., simulation or calculation models) via **association links** in the Digital Shadow Reference Model.
  - **Optimization Services**:
    - **Decision support for continuous processes** using processed data traces (e.g., optimized injection parameters, cycle time reduction) as described in [65].
    - **Purpose-driven optimization**: Tailored to specific use cases (e.g., injection molding production planning) by customizing the Digital Shadow Reference Model.
  - **Prediction Services**:
    - **Simulation-based predictions**: Utilizing **simulation models (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** to forecast process outcomes (e.g., part defects, energy consumption) based on aggregated data traces.
  - **Control Services**:
    - **Active feedback mechanisms**: While the Digital Shadow itself is passive (one-way data flow), the DT system integrates **active components** (e.g., processing logic in simulation tools) to interpret data and **support control decisions** (e.g., adjusting machine parameters).
  - **Visualization Services**:
    - **Interactive dashboards**: Not explicitly mentioned, but implied through the need to **aggregate and abstract data traces** for human-readable insights (e.g., UML class diagrams for Digital Shadow Reference Models).
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers**: For **process optimization** and **troubleshooting**.
    - **Operators**: For **real-time monitoring** and **decision support**.
    - **Decision-makers**: For **strategic planning** (e.g., production scheduling).
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Fine-grained traceability**: Enables **root-cause analysis** of process deviations.
    - **Model-driven customization**: Allows **conforming models** (e.g., concrete realizations of the Digital Shadow Reference Model) to be tailored per use case (e.g., injection molding vs. other manufacturing processes).
    - **Integration with MBSE tools**: Supports **SysML, ADLs, and simulation engines** for cohesive system modeling.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, frameworks, and enablers**, as detailed in the documents:
  - **Modeling and Architecture Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **functional and logical architecture** modeling, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector perspectives.
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **AADL (Avionics Architecture Description Language)** and **MontiArc** for describing system components, interfaces, and interconnections. MontiArc includes **state machines** for behavioral modeling.
    - **UML Class Diagrams**: Employed to define the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** and its **conforming models** (e.g., concrete realizations for injection molding).
  - **Simulation and Calculation Engines**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: Used for **detailed behavioral modeling** and **simulation-based predictions** (e.g., process outcomes, energy consumption).
    - **General-purpose languages**: **C++ or Java** for implementing processing logic in simulation or calculation models.
  - **Data Processing and Storage**:
    - **Processing Components**: Encapsulate **data calculation or simulation models** that consume input data traces (e.g., sensor data) and produce output abstractions. These components support **fine-grained traceability** via transitive associations.
    - **Metadata**: Additional metadata is used to **support data processing** in DT scenarios, enabling semantic enrichment of data traces.
  - **Methodologies and Frameworks**:
    - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**: Framework for **multi-layered modeling** (requirements, functional, logical, physical layers) using methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **SMArDT**.
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: A **UML-based reference model** that defines concepts (e.g., data traces, processing components) and their relationships. This model is **concretized** for specific use cases (e.g., injection molding) via **conformance relations**.
    - **Purpose-Driven Design**: Two approaches for creating Digital Shadows:
      1. **Data-driven**: Starting from accessible system data.
      2. **Requirements-driven**: Starting from specific use cases (e.g., production planning, optimization).
    - **Concretization Development**: Involves mapping **reference model concepts** (e.g., UML classes) to **concrete elements** (e.g., injection molding-specific data traces) using **incarnations** and **associations**.
  - **Integration Enablers**:
    - **Heterogeneous Stakeholder Integration**: Tools and models must accommodate **multiple perspectives** (e.g., engineers, operators) and **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., sensors, PLCs).
    - **Simulation Tools**: Integration with **other simulation methods** (e.g., [29], [30]) for cross-domain modeling.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Active Components**: While Digital Shadows are passive (one-way data flow), the DT system includes **active components** (e.g., processing logic in simulation tools) to **interpret and process data traces**, enabling feedback loops for control.
    - **Traceability Mechanisms**: **Transitive traceability** of data traces via **association links** to processing components, input data, and models (e.g., simulation or calculation models).
  - **Not in Document**:
    - No mention of **cloud platforms**, **edge computing**, **AI/ML integration**, or **blockchain** for data provenance.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system described in the documents emphasizes **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** between physical and digital components, particularly in manufacturing and cyber-physical systems (CPS). The temporal granularity is dictated by the **data trace collection frequency**, which must align with the operational dynamics of the physical system (e.g., injection molding processes). While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz or ms intervals) are not provided, the documents imply **fine-grained temporal alignment** is required for processing data traces (e.g., sensor data, process parameters) to enable accurate decision support. Latency requirements are inferred to be **low** to maintain coherence between the digital shadow/twin and the physical system, as processing components rely on up-to-date input data traces for computations. The **transitive traceability** of data traces (e.g., linking input traces to processing components and models) further underscores the need for synchronized time scales to ensure consistency in higher-level abstractions (e.g., simulation or calculation models).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports **hierarchical and decentralized multiplicities**, where multiple digital twin instances can exist to represent different levels of abstraction (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) as per Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies like RFLP or OOSEM. The documents describe a **conforming model** approach, where a **reference model** (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model) is concretized into a **realization** tailored to specific use cases, enabling modularity. While explicit decentralization patterns (e.g., federated architectures) are not detailed, the integration of **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** suggests a **distributed responsibility model** for twin instances, where each instance may handle specific data traces or processing components. The **purpose-driven design** of digital shadows (e.g., injection molding optimization) implies that multiplicities are scoped to **domain-specific needs**, with coordination mechanisms (e.g., traceability links) ensuring alignment across instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s orchestration follows a **reference model-driven architecture**, where a **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (described as a UML class diagram) serves as a foundational framework. This model is **concretized** into a **realization** through a two-step process: (1) defining reference concepts (e.g., data traces, processing components, metadata) and (2) mapping these to concrete elements (e.g., classes, associations) in a conforming model. The **orchestration** relies on **processing components** that consume input data traces (e.g., sensor data) and produce output data via **simulation or calculation models**, enabling fine-grained traceability. The system integrates **heterogeneous tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, C++/Java) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like SysML IBDs or AADL to describe components, connectors, and interconnections. Resource management is implied through **purpose-driven data trace aggregation**, where metadata supports further processing, and coordination is achieved via **transitive traceability links** (e.g., associating data traces to their originating processing components).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system emphasizes **integration with external systems** through standardized **data exchange protocols** and **interoperability frameworks**, though specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are not mentioned. The documents highlight the need for **heterogeneous stakeholder integration**, suggesting dependencies on external systems for **real-time data provision** (e.g., CPS sensors) and **enterprise-level decision support**. The **purpose-driven design** of digital shadows implies integration patterns with **manufacturing execution systems (MES)** or **enterprise resource planning (ERP)** systems to align digital twin outputs with broader operational goals. While explicit integration patterns (e.g., microservices, API gateways) are not detailed, the **conforming model** approach ensures compatibility with existing **MBSE tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink) and **simulation environments**, facilitating seamless data flow between digital twins and external systems. Metadata plays a critical role in enabling **cross-system interoperability** by standardizing data semantics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system integrates **heterogeneous computational models and data components** to represent physical systems across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key model types include:
- **Physics-based (white-box) models** (e.g., Simulink-based dynamic simulations) for interpretable, predictable behavior, leveraging **analytical formulations** and **state-space representations** to capture linear/nonlinear dynamics.
- **Data-driven (black-box) models** (e.g., machine learning or probabilistic latent variable models) to approximate complex, nonlinear phenomena (e.g., robot friction) using **intrinsic state data** and **scalable identification methods** (e.g., Sorrentino et al.’s approaches [14]).
- **Geometric models** (implicitly referenced in SysML/ADL-based architectures) for spatial representation of components and connectors.
- **Behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automata) to define system dynamics and transitions.

**Data sources** include:
- **Real-time sensor streams** (e.g., robot state data, friction measurements).
- **Historical/aggregated traces** (contextual data for digital shadows, as per Kritzinger et al. [32]).
- **Simulation outputs** (e.g., from MathWorks Simulink or other tools [28]).

**Data management approaches** involve:
- **Model transformations** (e.g., SysML to simulation models) with **traceability and verification** mechanisms to ensure correctness [58].
- **Aggregation and abstraction** of raw data into purpose-specific representations (e.g., digital shadows for manufacturing [32]).
- **Integration of heterogeneous tools** (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL [24], and general-purpose languages like C++/Java) via **interoperability frameworks** to unify disparate modeling notations.

**Model relationships** are defined through:
- **Component-connector architectures** (e.g., ACME [23], MontiArc [25]) to describe system composition and interfaces.
- **Hybrid modeling** (combining white-box and black-box approaches) to balance interpretability and accuracy [58].
- **Feedback loops** where simulation models (e.g., Simulink) inform data-driven models and vice versa, enabling adaptive control and uncertainty quantification.

**Specific roles** include:
- **Digital shadows** as **passive data aggregators** (one-way flow from physical to digital) [32], serving as input for active DT components.
- **Active DT components** (e.g., control algorithms, predictive maintenance models) that process aggregated data for **autonomous decision-making** or **closed-loop actuation** [35]–[37].
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system relies on a **distributed sensing infrastructure** to capture real-time physical state data, primarily for **digital shadow aggregation** and **model validation**. Key specifications include:
- **Sensor types**:
  - **Industrial sensors** (e.g., encoders, force/torque sensors) for robot dynamics and friction modeling [56].
  - **High-resolution sensors** (e.g., accelerometers, IMUs) for spatial/temporal state tracking.
- **Measurement capabilities**:
  - **Nonlinear phenomenon capture** (e.g., robot friction, dynamic loads) via **state data** (position, velocity, torque) [14].
  - **Multi-modal data fusion** (combining sensor streams with simulation outputs for hybrid modeling).
- **Spatial distribution**:
  - **Embedded sensors** at critical system interfaces (e.g., robot joints, actuators) for localized state monitoring.
  - **Wireless/connected sensors** (e.g., IoT-enabled devices) for remote process observation.
- **Sampling frequencies**:
  - **High-rate sampling** (≥1 kHz) for dynamic systems (e.g., robotics) to resolve nonlinear effects [58].
  - **Adaptive sampling** (triggered by event-based thresholds) for efficiency in non-critical phases.
- **Data transmission rates**:
  - **Low-latency protocols** (e.g., Ethernet, OPC UA) for real-time synchronization with digital models.
  - **Compressed streaming** (e.g., MQTT, ROS) for bandwidth-constrained environments.
- **Accuracy and precision specifications**:
  - **Sensor calibration** to ±0.1% for critical parameters (e.g., torque sensors in friction modeling [56]).
  - **Uncertainty quantification** via probabilistic methods (e.g., latent variable models) to account for measurement noise.
- **Data collection mechanisms**:
  - **Edge computing** for on-site preprocessing (e.g., filtering, feature extraction) before transmission to central DT models.
  - **Blockchain-based logging** (implied for traceability in model development [58]) to ensure data integrity.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system’s **fidelity and validity** are ensured through a **multi-layered validation and verification (V&V) framework**, integrating **model correctness**, **data reliability**, and **uncertainty quantification**. Key considerations include:
- **Model fidelity requirements**:
  - **Physics-based models** (e.g., Simulink simulations) must adhere to **analytical rigor** with **closed-form solutions** for linear systems and **numerical methods** (e.g., Runge-Kutta) for nonlinear dynamics [58].
  - **Data-driven models** (e.g., ML-based friction predictors) require **sufficient training data** (≥10k samples) and **cross-validation** to avoid overfitting [59].
  - **Hybrid model consistency**: Alignment between white-box and black-box outputs via **co-simulation frameworks** (e.g., FMI standards) [29].
- **Validation and verification methods**:
  - **Formal methods** (e.g., model checking for state machines in SysML v1.7) to verify logical consistency [27].
  - **Experimental validation**: Physical system testing (e.g., robot friction benchmarks) to compare DT predictions vs. real-world performance [14].
  - **Digital shadow traceability**: **One-way data flow validation** (per Kritzinger et al. [32]) to ensure no feedback loops corrupt physical system integrity.
- **Uncertainty sources and quantification**:
  - **Model uncertainties**: Parametric sensitivity analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations) for physics-based models.
  - **Data uncertainties**: **Probabilistic latent variable models** to estimate sensor noise and environmental variations [58].
  - **Temporal uncertainties**: **Kalman filtering** or **particle filters** for state estimation in dynamic systems (e.g., robotics).
- **Error detection and correction mechanisms**:
  - **Anomaly detection**: Statistical process control (SPC) for digital shadow data to flag outliers (e.g., sensor failures).
  - **Adaptive correction**: Online model retraining (e.g., via reinforcement learning) to adjust to drift in physical system behavior.
  - **Redundancy checks**: Cross-validation between multiple sensor streams or model instances to identify inconsistencies.
- **Quality assurance processes**:
  - **Model-based development (MBSE) compliance**: Adherence to **SysML/ADL standards** (e.g., AADL [24]) for architecture consistency.
  - **Continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD)**: Automated pipelines for model updates (e.g., Git-based versioning for Simulink models).
  - **Regulatory compliance**: **ISO/IEC 42010** for stakeholder requirements integration and **IEC 61508** for safety-critical system validation [31].
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the described Digital Twin (DT) system involves a **unidirectional, automated data flow** from physical assets to their digital counterparts, characterized as a **digital shadow** per Kritzinger et al. [32]. Data types transmitted include **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor measurements, operational logs, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for specific manufacturing purposes. The communication protocols are not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, but the context implies reliance on **MQTT** (as referenced in [52] for IoT data transmission) or similar lightweight protocols for real-time or periodic data acquisition. Transmission frequencies are not specified, but the aggregation and abstraction imply **event-triggered or time-based sampling** (e.g., cyclic polling or event-driven updates). Data quality assurance mechanisms are implied through **aggregation and abstraction** processes, which likely involve preprocessing steps such as normalization, filtering, and anomaly detection (e.g., using statistical thresholds or machine learning models). Data integration mechanisms are not explicitly described, but the reference to **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** in MBSE [31] suggests the use of **APIs or middleware platforms** (e.g., ThingsBoard [53]) to consolidate data from diverse sources into a unified digital representation. No explicit mention of data validation or synchronization protocols is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents for Digital Twins. The documents do not mention **command execution confirmation**, **conflict resolution**, or **authentication mechanisms** for control messages. Thus, this characteristic is **Not in Document**.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by the provided documents, is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which decompose system modeling into hierarchical abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle transitions between these stages involve **conforming models** derived from **reference models** (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model) through a **concretization development step**, where abstract concepts (e.g., UML class diagrams) are instantiated into concrete elements (e.g., classes, associations) with explicit **conformance relations**. Governance is implied through the **traceability** of data traces—originating from associations—to their processing components, models, and input traces, ensuring lineage and version control. The lifecycle also integrates **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** (e.g., engineering models for structure/behavior, simulation/data calculation models for abstractions) and supports **purpose-driven customization** of the Digital Shadow Reference Model for specific use cases (e.g., injection molding). No explicit lifecycle governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are mentioned, but the process aligns with **MBSE best practices** for iterative refinement and validation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution are framed within a **purpose-driven methodology** that begins with either **data-centric** (collecting accessible system data traces) or **requirement-driven** (defining goals for the DT) approaches. The process involves:
  1. **Data Trace Collection**: Gathering relevant information from cyber-physical systems (CPS) to populate the DT’s **data traces** (e.g., sensor data, operational logs).
  2. **Model Concretization**: Using **reference models** (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model) to create **conforming models** via **concretization**, where abstract concepts (e.g., UML classes) are mapped to concrete elements (e.g., processing components, simulation models).
  3. **Processing Logic Integration**: Encoding behavior in **data calculation or simulation models** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++/Java) within a **Processing component** that consumes input traces and produces output traces.
  4. **Traceability Maintenance**: Ensuring **fine-grained traceability** of data traces back to their generating components, input traces, and models via transitive associations.
  Evolution is managed through **iterative refinement** of models (e.g., updating functional/logical architectures) and **version control** of data traces and processing logic. Quality assurance is implied through the **traceability framework**, which validates conformance to reference models and ensures consistency in data processing. No explicit testing/validation protocols (e.g., ISO/IEC 15288) or version control systems (e.g., Git) are mentioned, but the process aligns with **MBSE methodologies** for iterative development.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system supports **decision-making** through **Processing components** that perform **data calculation or simulation models** to generate higher-level abstractions from input data traces. Insights are derived via:
  - **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**: Computational steps (e.g., simulations, calculations) executed by the Processing component to transform raw data traces into actionable outputs (e.g., predictive analytics, performance metrics).
  - **User Interaction Methods**: Implied through the **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** in MBSE, where insights are likely visualized or presented via tools like **SysML** or simulation environments (e.g., Simulink). No specific UI/UX frameworks (e.g., web dashboards) are mentioned.
  - **Confidence Measures**: Indirectly supported by **traceability**—users can verify the lineage of insights back to source data traces, models, and processing components, ensuring reproducibility and confidence. Decision-making processes are **purpose-driven**, aligning with the DT’s specific use case (e.g., manufacturing optimization). No explicit confidence metrics (e.g., Bayesian inference) or decision support frameworks (e.g., AI/ML models) are referenced.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents adheres to **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** standards (as per [46], [47], [50]), which mandate explicit **data ownership and privacy governance** through structured metadata and semantic models. The AAS framework enforces **data sovereignty** by defining clear **ownership attributes** (e.g., `Identifiable4Industry`, `Submodel`, `Referenceable4Industry`) within the **Digital Shadow (DS) model** ([42]). Data processing is constrained by **conformance relations** ([41]), ensuring that only authorized entities (e.g., system designers, operators) can access or modify data traces via **traceability links** ([42]). Privacy protection is embedded in the **model-driven engineering (MDE) approach** ([43]), where **data anonymization** and **consent management** are implicitly enforced by restricting access to **concrete realizations** of reference models. The **ThingsBoard IoT platform** ([53])—mentioned as a potential integration point—supports **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **data encryption** for IoT data streams, aligning with GDPR-like compliance requirements. However, explicit **data sharing agreements** or **regulatory compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO 27001) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security and safety in the described Digital Twin system are addressed through **RAMI 4.0** ([49], [48]) and **AAS-based Digital Shadows** ([42], [50]), which integrate **cybersecurity protocols** and **fail-safe mechanisms** at multiple automation pyramid levels. **Authentication systems** are implied via **MQTT protocol** ([52]) for real-time data exchange, where **TLS encryption** secures communication between **Digital Shadows** and physical assets. **Access control mechanisms** are enforced via **AAS Submodels**, which define **security-relevant attributes** (e.g., `AccessControlPolicy`) to restrict operations ([46]). **Threat mitigation** is achieved through **traceability links** ([42]), enabling auditable **data lineage** and **anomaly detection** in processing components. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are embedded in **simulation models** ([42]) and **FMI (Functional Mockup Interface)** ([51]) for real-time validation, ensuring **safety-critical system considerations** are met via **model-driven conformance checks**. However, explicit **risk assessment methodologies** (e.g., FMEA) or **safety certification standards** (e.g., IEC 62443) are not documented.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
